_schema: Major documented geomagnetic and solar events in the historical record. Each entry is hand-curated from peer-reviewed literature and agency reports; see the `sources` array for each event. Dates are UTC. The Dst index was formalized in 1957; events before then carry reconstructed values from magnetogram archives (Kew, Greenwich, Colaba, Göttingen) with order-of-magnitude uncertainty, while 1957+ events carry the measured minimum Dst from Kyoto WDC. The `dst_source` field makes the distinction explicit. Curator: HelioChronicles. Last reviewed 2026-04.

_fields.name: common name, as it appears in the literature
_fields.date_start: ISO date (UTC) of onset — usually the driver (flare/CME) or first storm activity
_fields.date_end: ISO date (UTC) of primary storm end; same as start for single-day events
_fields.flare_class_peak: X-ray class of the associated or driving flare (string, e.g. 'X28+'); null if pre-satellite
_fields.dst_nT: minimum Dst in nT (negative); null when no Dst value exists. Pair with dst_source to know measurement provenance.
_fields.dst_source: measured (Kyoto WDC, 1957+) | reconstructed (pre-1957 magnetogram archives) | estimated-hypothetical (CME missed Earth; value is a model projection) | null
_fields.storm_scale: NOAA G-scale class (G1..G5); null for historical events outside the modern scale
_fields.aurora_lat_deg: lowest reported geomagnetic latitude of visible aurora, approximate
_fields.cycle: solar cycle number at the time of the event
_fields.source_region_ids: list of ids in data/regions/notable_regions.json that produced this event; empty array when the driver region is unknown (pre-numbering era) or ambiguous (backside / limb crossings)
_fields.sources: list of citations — papers, agency reports, observational logs

events.name: Carrington Event
events.significance: The benchmark extreme solar event. Aurora observed in the Caribbean; telegraph systems failed or operated without battery power from induced currents.
effects: Telegraph operators shocked; paper records caught fire
Auroras reported in Cuba, Colombia, Hawaii, and central Mexico
Magnetograms at Kew (London) and Colaba (Bombay) went off-scale

sources.citation: Carrington, R. C. (1859). Description of a Singular Appearance seen in the Sun on September 1, 1859. MNRAS 20, 13.
Cliver, E. W. & Dietrich, W. F. (2013). The 1859 space weather event revisited: limits of extreme activity. J. Space Weather Space Clim. 3, A31.
Tsurutani, B. T. et al. (2003). The extreme magnetic storm of 1–2 September 1859. JGR 108, 1268.

events.name: Great Magnetic Storm of February 1872
events.significance: Aurora seen from Mumbai, Khartoum, and Santiago. Among the lowest-latitude aurora records in history; comparable in intensity to Carrington.
effects: Aurora logs from Egypt, India, Chile, and Japan
Telegraph disruption across North America and Europe

sources.citation: Hayakawa, H. et al. (2018). The Great Space Weather Event during 1872 February Recorded in East Asia. ApJ 862, 15.

events.name: New York Railroad Storm
events.significance: Largest storm of the 20th century before 1989. Railroad and telegraph systems across the US and Europe failed; a fire at Central New England Railroad's control tower was attributed to induced currents.
effects: Signal and telegraph fires in New York and Sweden
Aurora reported as far south as Pasadena, CA and Samoa
Transatlantic cable traffic halted

sources.citation: Hapgood, M. (2019). The Great Storm of May 1921: An Exemplar of a Dangerous Space Weather Event. Space Weather 17, 950–975.
Love, J. J. et al. (2019). On the intensity of the magnetic superstorm of September 1909. Space Weather 17, 37–45.

events.name: Easter Sunday Storm
events.significance: First major storm of the instrumental Ap era; reference point for 20th-century storm characterization.
effects: Long-distance telephone and radio service disrupted
Aurora visible across the continental US

sources.citation: Lanzerotti, L. J. (2017). Space weather strikes. Physics Today 70, 7, 9.

events.name: 23 February 1956 Ground-Level Enhancement
events.significance: The largest solar proton event in the modern record (GLE #5). Reference event for radiation-hardening standards.
effects: Neutron monitor counts increased by ~4500% at mid-latitudes
Still the benchmark proton event for spacecraft shielding analysis

sources.citation: Meyer, P., Parker, E. N. & Simpson, J. A. (1956). Solar Cosmic Rays of February 1956 and their Propagation through Interplanetary Space. Phys. Rev. 104, 768.

events.name: August 1972 Event
events.significance: Fastest transit time to Earth (14.6 hours) for any recorded CME. Occurred between Apollo 16 and Apollo 17; radiation dose would have been fatal or near-fatal to astronauts on the lunar surface.
effects: Detonated dozens of U.S. Navy magnetic-influence sea mines off Hai Phong harbor
Radio and power-grid disturbances in North America
Declassified in 2018 as a U.S. Navy operational incident

sources.citation: Knipp, D. J. et al. (2018). On the Little-Known Consequences of the 4 August 1972 Ultra-Fast Coronal Mass Ejecta. Space Weather 16, 1635–1643.

events.name: Quebec Blackout Storm
events.significance: Collapsed the Hydro-Québec grid in 90 seconds; 6 million people lost power for up to 9 hours. The modern canonical 'real-world damage' geomagnetic storm.
effects: Hydro-Québec system blackout, ~$2 B CAD in damage and recovery
Transformer damage at the Salem Nuclear Plant, New Jersey
Aurora visible in Florida, Cuba, and Texas

sources.citation: Boteler, D. H. (2019). A 21st Century View of the March 1989 Magnetic Storm. Space Weather 17, 1427–1441.
North American Electric Reliability Corporation (1990). March 13, 1989 Geomagnetic Disturbance.

events.name: Bastille Day Event
events.significance: Among the first fully space-instrumented major events; provided the modern CME-timing template used by operational space-weather forecasts.
effects: Spacecraft anomalies on ASCA (lost), GOES, and SOHO
Polar-route airline rerouting
GPS accuracy degraded by tens of meters

sources.citation: Smith, Z. et al. (2003). A comparison of the bastille day event (2000 July 14) with the Halloween storm (2003 October 29). Solar Phys. 225, 377.
NOAA SWPC event report, July 2000.

events.name: Halloween Storms
events.significance: Two X17 flares and one X28+ flare (off-scale, largest ever directly observed) inside two weeks; the largest multi-storm sequence of the satellite era.
effects: Swedish city of Malmö lost power (~50,000 customers)
Mars Odyssey's MARIE radiation instrument permanently damaged
Airlines rerouted across polar corridors for a week
FAA declared the first-ever space-weather flight diversion advisory

sources.citation: Gopalswamy, N. et al. (2005). Solar sources and geospace consequences of intense geomagnetic storms in October–November 2003. J. Geophys. Res. 110, A09S15.
NOAA SWPC (2004). Halloween storms event review.

events.name: September 2005 X17 Flare Series
events.significance: The most flare-productive active region (AR10808) of SC23, producing nine X-class flares in a week.
effects: Short-wave radio blackouts lasting hours
GPS position errors up to tens of meters

sources.citation: NOAA SWPC event archive, September 2005.

events.name: July 2012 Near-Miss CME
events.significance: STEREO-A measured a CME whose solar-wind speed at 1 AU reached ~2200 km/s — Carrington-class. Earth was ahead of the ejection direction by ~9 days; a direct hit would have produced the largest storm of the instrumental era.
effects: No terrestrial effects — Earth was not in the path
Observed only because STEREO-A was ahead of Earth in its orbit

sources.citation: Baker, D. N. et al. (2013). A major solar eruptive event in July 2012: Defining extreme space weather scenarios. Space Weather 11, 585–591.
Liu, Y. D. et al. (2014). Observations of an extreme storm in interplanetary space caused by successive coronal mass ejections. Nat. Commun. 5, 3481.

events.name: September 2017 Flares and Storm
events.significance: Largest flare of SC24 (X9.3, September 6); reminder that weak cycles can still produce extreme events. Occurred near solar minimum, against forecast expectations.
effects: Radio blackouts across the sunlit side of Earth
Amateur radio and aviation HF disrupted during Atlantic hurricane response

sources.citation: Redmon, R. J. et al. (2018). September 2017's geoeffective space weather and impacts to caribbean radio communications during hurricane response. Space Weather 16, 1190–1201.

events.name: Gannon Storm (Mother's Day Storm)
events.significance: First G5 storm since Halloween 2003. Named posthumously for space-weather scientist Jennifer Gannon. Produced aurora across all 50 US states, widespread sightings at subtropical latitudes.
effects: ~40% of SpaceX Starlink satellites experienced increased drag, some deorbited early
GPS positional errors up to meters for days
Aurora photographed from Puerto Rico, Mexico, Kenya, and India
No major power-grid outages reported

sources.citation: NOAA SWPC (2024). Mother's Day Geomagnetic Storm.
Parker, W. E. & Linares, R. (2024). Satellite Drag Analysis During the May 2024 Gannon Geomagnetic Storm. AIAA preprint.

events.name: October 2024 G4 Storm
events.significance: Second major SC25 storm within 5 months of Gannon. Lower-latitude aurora across continental Europe, North America, and northern Africa.
effects: Aurora visible from Florida, southern France, and Greece
Short-wave radio blackouts across Americas

sources.citation: NOAA SWPC (2024). October 2024 G4 storm event summary.
